question: Einstein was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for:
information: Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his explanation of the photoelectric effect (not relativity, as many assume). This work laid the foundation for quantum mechanics. His famous equation: E = hν (energy of photon = Planck's constant × frequency).

question: The fundamental (SI) unit of luminous intensity is:
information: Candela is one of the 7 base SI units measuring luminous intensity — the power of light emitted per unit solid angle. Lux measures illuminance (light falling on a surface) and is a derived unit.

question: Weber is the SI unit of:
information: Weber (Wb) is the SI unit of magnetic flux. 1 Weber = 1 Volt·second. The SI unit of magnetic field strength (B) is Tesla (T), where 1 T = 1 Wb/m².

question: The prefix 'femto' stands for:
information: Femto (f) = 10⁻¹⁵. Memory aid: pico (10⁻¹²) → femto (10⁻¹⁵) → atto (10⁻¹⁸). Femtometre is used to measure nuclear dimensions.

question: How many base (fundamental) SI units are there?
information: The 7 SI base units are: metre (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), candela (luminous intensity).

question: Light-year is a unit of:
information: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year ≈ 9.46 × 10¹⁵ metres. Used in astronomy. It is NOT a unit of time despite the word 'year' — a common BPSC trap.

question: Ampere is the SI unit of:
information: Ampere (A) is one of the 7 base SI units measuring electric current. Electric charge is in Coulombs (C) where 1 C = 1 A × 1 s. Resistance is in Ohms (Ω).

question: The SI unit of pressure is:
information: Pascal (Pa) = 1 Newton per square metre (N/m²). Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa = 1 atm. Bar = 10⁵ Pa (used in meteorology). Named after Blaise Pascal.

question: Decibel is the unit of:
information: Decibel (dB) measures sound intensity on a logarithmic scale. Threshold of hearing = 0 dB; normal conversation ≈ 60 dB; pain threshold ≈ 120-130 dB. Noise pollution limits are set in dB.

question: The SI unit of electric charge is:
information: Coulomb (C) is the SI unit of electric charge. 1 Coulomb = charge carried by ~6.24 × 10¹⁸ electrons. Named after French physicist Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.

question: One Astronomical Unit (AU) is the average distance between:
information: 1 AU ≈ 149.6 million km (1.496 × 10¹¹ m). Used to express distances within the Solar System.

question: Which physical quantity has no unit (dimensionless)?
information: Strain = change in length / original length — a pure ratio with no units. Other dimensionless quantities: refractive index, relative density, coefficient of friction.

question: One Newton equals:
information: From Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma. Units: kg × m/s² = kg·m·s⁻². Force needed to accelerate 1 kg mass at 1 m/s².

question: The SI unit of acceleration is:
information: Acceleration = change in velocity / time = (m/s)/s = m/s². Acceleration due to gravity on Earth ≈ 9.8 m/s².

question: The SI unit of electric resistance is:
information: Ohm (Ω) is defined by Ohm's Law: R = V/I. 1 Ohm = 1 Volt/Ampere. Conductance (inverse of resistance) is measured in Siemens (S).

question: 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) equals:
information: 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ. This is the unit in electricity bills. 1 unit of electricity = 1 kWh.

question: Electric potential is measured in:
information: Electric potential (V) = Work done per unit charge = J/C. 1 Volt = 1 J/C. The potential difference between two points drives electric current.

question: Which of the following pairs have the same SI units?
information: Work = Force × displacement (Joule = N·m). Energy is also measured in Joules. Power = Energy/time (Watt = J/s). Force = N. Pressure = N/m².

question: The SI unit of power is:
information: Watt (W) = Joule/second. 1 HP = 746 W. Named after James Watt.

question: One horsepower is approximately equal to:
information: 1 HP (mechanical horsepower) = 745.7 W ≈ 746 W. Still used for motors, vehicles, and pumps. Coined by James Watt to compare steam engine output with horse power.

question: Ohm-metre (Ω·m) is the unit of:
information: Resistivity (ρ) is the intrinsic resistance of a material per unit length and unit cross-section. R = ρL/A. Copper has very low resistivity; ideal for wires.

question: Angstrom (Å) is a unit of:
information: 1 Angstrom = 10⁻¹⁰ m. Used for atomic/molecular dimensions and wavelengths of light. Visible light: ~4000–7000 Å.

question: What is the value of the solar constant?
information: Solar constant ≈ 1.361–1.4 kW/m² — solar radiation per unit area at Earth's outer atmosphere. Basis for solar energy calculations.

question: What is measured in Hertz (Hz)?
information: Hertz (Hz) = cycles per second. Measures frequency. Radio waves: MHz–GHz. Audible sound: 20 Hz–20 kHz. Named after Heinrich Hertz.

question: What is the normal hearing frequency range of humans?
information: Humans hear 20 Hz (low bass) to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz). Below 20 Hz = infrasound (elephants use this). Above 20 kHz = ultrasound (bats, dogs). Hearing range decreases with age.

question: Newton's First Law of Motion is also known as the Law of:
information: Newton's First Law: a body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion unless acted upon by an external net force. Inertia = resistance to change in state of motion. Example: passengers lurch forward when bus brakes suddenly.

question: Which of Newton's Laws gives the mathematical definition of force?
information: Newton's Second Law: F = ma (Force = Mass × Acceleration). This law defines force quantitatively. SI unit of force is Newton (N) = kg·m/s². First Law defines inertia; Third Law defines action-reaction pairs.

question: A rocket works on the principle of which of Newton's Laws?
information: Rockets use Newton's Third Law: hot gases expelled backward (action) push the rocket forward (reaction). This works even in vacuum (unlike jet engines which need air). Basis of all rocket propulsion including ISRO's PSLV and GSLV.

question: The weight of a body on the Moon is approximately:
information: Moon's gravitational acceleration ≈ 1.6 m/s² = 1/6th of Earth's (9.8 m/s²). Since Weight = mg, weight on Moon = 1/6 of Earth weight. Mass remains unchanged everywhere.

question: The escape velocity from Earth's surface is approximately:
information: Escape velocity from Earth = 11.2 km/s = minimum speed needed to escape Earth's gravity permanently. Orbital velocity (for satellites in LEO) = 7.9 km/s. Speed of light = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Speed of sound ≈ 340 m/s.

question: According to the Law of Conservation of Energy:
answer: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed
choices: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
Kinetic energy always equals potential energy
information: The Law of Conservation of Energy: total energy of an isolated system remains constant — energy transforms from one form to another (PE → KE → heat) but total amount never changes. First Law of Thermodynamics.

question: A see-saw is an example of which type of lever?
information: First-class lever: fulcrum between load and effort (e.g., see-saw, scissors, crowbar). Second-class: load between effort and fulcrum (wheelbarrow, nutcracker). Third-class: effort between fulcrum and load (tweezers, forearm bicep).

question: Pressure in physics is defined as:
information: Pressure (P) = Force (F) / Area (A). Unit: Pascal (Pa) = N/m². For same force, smaller area → higher pressure (knife edge vs flat surface). Blood pressure, atmospheric pressure, tyre pressure are all examples.

question: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
information: Scalars have magnitude only; vectors have both magnitude and direction. Mass is scalar. Weight, momentum, and acceleration are vectors. Other scalars: speed, distance, energy, temperature.

question: Which of the following is NOT a vector quantity?
information: Speed is scalar (magnitude only). Velocity is vector (speed + direction). Other scalars: distance, mass, energy, temperature. Vectors: force, acceleration, momentum, torque, displacement.

question: Current density is:
information: Current density (J) = current per unit cross-sectional area (A/m²). Has both magnitude and direction (direction of positive charge flow) — making it a vector quantity.

question: The rate of change of momentum of a body is equal to:
information: Newton's Second Law: F = dp/dt = rate of change of momentum. Impulse = Force × time = change in momentum (not rate of change). Longer stopping time (airbag, goalkeeper) reduces force.

question: Which is the correct formula of acceleration?
information: Acceleration = (Final velocity − Initial velocity) / time. Kinematic equations: v = u + at; s = ut + ½at²; v² = u² + 2as.

question: Force is the product of:
information: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. Unit: Newton (N) = kg·m/s². Velocity is not part of this — that's momentum (p = mv).

question: Which physical quantity is represented by the ratio of momentum to velocity?
information: Momentum (p) = mass × velocity. So p/v = m. This is a good conceptual question: momentum/velocity = mass. Similarly, force/acceleration = mass (Newton's 2nd law).

question: Ball bearings are used to convert static friction into:
information: Ball bearings replace sliding contact with rolling contact, converting static/sliding friction into much smaller rolling friction. Rolling friction < sliding friction < static friction.

question: Which energy conversion is primarily involved when frictional force acts?
information: Friction converts kinetic energy into heat. Why rubbing hands produces warmth, brakes heat up, meteors glow on entering atmosphere. Friction is always a dissipative force.

question: Lenz's law is derived from the law of conservation of:
information: Lenz's law states that induced current opposes the change causing it — ensuring energy is conserved. If it aided the change, energy would be created from nothing — violating conservation.

question: If a feather, a rubber ball, and a wooden ball fall freely in vacuum from the same height:
answer: All three reach the ground together
choices: All three reach the ground together
information: In vacuum, no air resistance. All objects fall with same acceleration (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²) regardless of mass or shape — demonstrated by Galileo and Apollo 15 astronauts on the Moon.

question: When a body falls freely towards the Earth, its total mechanical energy:
information: During free fall, potential energy converts to kinetic energy, but total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant — Law of Conservation of Energy.

question: The time period of a simple pendulum depends on:
information: T = 2π√(L/g). Time period depends on length (L) and g — NOT on mass or amplitude (for small angles). Longer pendulum swings more slowly. This is why grandfather clocks are tall.

question: For a body moving with constant speed in a horizontal circle, which remains constant?
information: Speed is constant → KE = ½mv² is constant. But velocity direction keeps changing → centripetal acceleration is not zero. The centripetal force keeps changing direction (always towards centre).

question: If the spinning speed of the Earth increases, the weight of a body at the equator will:
information: Weight at equator = mg − mω²R. If ω increases, centrifugal effect increases, so effective weight decreases. At the poles, spinning has no effect on weight.

question: When standing in an elevator coming down with acceleration = g (9.8 m/s²), your apparent weight is:
information: N = m(g−a) = m(9.8−9.8) = 0. You experience weightlessness — same as free fall. Principle behind simulated zero gravity in aircraft (parabolic flights).

question: A bus takes a sharp right turn suddenly. The passengers will:
information: Newton's First Law (Inertia): passengers tend to continue in their original direction. When bus turns right, passengers' bodies continue straight — they lean left. This is inertia, not centrifugal force.

question: A goalkeeper pulls hands backwards after catching the ball. This helps to:
answer: Decrease the rate of change of momentum (reduce force on hands)
choices: Decrease the rate of change of momentum (reduce force on hands)
Increase the force exerted by the ball on hands
Exert large force on the ball
information: Impulse = Force × time = change in momentum. By increasing stopping time (pulling back), the same impulse is delivered over longer period, reducing force. Same principle: airbags, crumple zones, padded flooring.

question: When a stone is brought from the Moon's surface to Earth:
answer: Its weight will change, but mass will not
choices: Its weight will change, but mass will not
Both weight and mass will change
information: Mass is intrinsic — same everywhere. Weight = mg — changes with gravity. Moon's g ≈ 1/6th of Earth's. So stone weighs 6× more on Earth. Mass = same.

question: For a satellite, escape velocity is 11 km/s. If launched at 60° with the vertical, escape velocity will:
information: Escape velocity depends only on the mass of the planet and distance from centre — NOT on the direction of launch. Always √(2gR) = 11.2 km/s for Earth.

question: The potential energy of a freely falling body continuously decreases. This means:
answer: The principle of conservation of energy is NOT violated
choices: The principle of conservation of energy is NOT violated
information: As PE decreases, KE increases by equal amount. Total mechanical energy (PE + KE) remains constant. Energy merely transforms — not created or destroyed.

question: A pair of oxen exerts a force of 140 N while ploughing a field 15 m long. Work done is:
information: Work = Force × Displacement = 140 × 15 = 2,100 J. Valid when force and displacement are in the same direction.

question: We apply 200 N force to push a box on the floor at constant velocity. The friction force is:
information: Constant velocity → zero acceleration → net force = 0 → friction = applied force = 200 N. This is kinetic friction exactly balancing the applied force.

question: A truck starting from rest achieves 400 m in 20 s. If mass = 7 tonnes, force acting on it is:
Energy consumed = 250 kWh (units). Total energy in joules:
A sudden rise in barometric pressure (reading of barometer) indicates:
information: A sudden/abnormal rise in barometric pressure typically precedes a storm or cyclone. Normally: rising pressure = fair weather; falling = rain/storms. But a sudden sharp rise is a warning sign of an approaching storm.

question: The time period of a second's pendulum is:
information: A second's pendulum has a time period of 2 seconds (1 second for each swing/half-period). Its length on Earth ≈ 99.4 cm ≈ 1 metre. Used in grandfather clocks.

question: A man exerts maximum pressure on the Earth when:
information: Pressure = Force/Area. When lying down, contact area is maximum → pressure is MINIMUM. When standing, area (foot soles) is much smaller → higher pressure. A person on stilts exerts even more pressure.

question: The focal length of a plane mirror is:
information: A plane mirror has radius of curvature = infinity (flat surface), so focal length = R/2 = ∞. A plane mirror neither converges nor diverges light — only reflects it.

question: Which type of mirror is used as a rear-view mirror in vehicles?
information: Convex mirrors give a wider field of view (see more area) and always form erect, diminished, virtual images. Concave mirrors are used in headlights, solar cookers, and shaving/makeup mirrors.

question: Optical fibres work on the principle of:
information: Optical fibres use Total Internal Reflection — when light travels from denser to rarer medium at an angle greater than the critical angle, it reflects back completely. Allows data (as light) to travel with minimal loss. Basis of internet broadband.

question: The rainbow is formed due to:
answer: Dispersion, Refraction, and Total Internal Reflection inside water droplets
choices: Dispersion, Refraction, and Total Internal Reflection inside water droplets
Scattering of light by air molecules
information: A rainbow: sunlight enters water droplets → refracts → internally reflects → disperses into colours → refracts again on exit. Red on outside (~42°), violet on inside (~40°) of primary rainbow.

question: Which colour of light has the longest wavelength?
information: In visible spectrum (VIBGYOR): Red has longest wavelength (~700 nm), violet has shortest (~400 nm). Red scatters least — which is why it is used in traffic signals and danger signals (visible from far).

question: A person suffering from Myopia (short-sightedness) should use:
information: Myopia: cannot see distant objects clearly (image forms in front of retina). Corrected by concave (diverging) lens. Hypermetropia (long-sightedness): cannot see near objects (image forms behind retina) — corrected by convex (converging) lens.

question: The sky appears blue because:
answer: Blue light scatters more than other colours (Rayleigh scattering)
choices: Blue light scatters more than other colours (Rayleigh scattering)
Water vapour absorbs all other colours
information: Air molecules scatter shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) more than longer wavelengths (red). Our eyes are more sensitive to blue than violet, so sky appears blue — Rayleigh scattering. At sunset, red/orange colours dominate (light travels longer path through atmosphere).

question: In which medium does sound travel the fastest?
information: Speed of sound: solids > liquids > gases. Cannot travel in vacuum (mechanical wave needs medium). Steel ≈ 5100 m/s; water ≈ 1500 m/s; air ≈ 343 m/s. Closely packed molecules in solids transfer vibration faster.

question: Ultrasound refers to sound waves with frequency:
information: Ultrasound: frequency > 20 kHz (above human hearing range). Used in: medical sonography (imaging), SONAR (submarine detection), metal crack detection, industrial cleaning. Infrasound: < 20 Hz (used by elephants, whales).

question: Which of the following radiation is used for relief from body aches?
information: Infrared radiation penetrates skin and heats body tissues, increasing blood flow and relieving muscle aches and joint pain. IR lamps are used in physiotherapy. UV radiation is used for sterilisation.

question: Bolometer is used to measure:
information: A bolometer measures the power of incident electromagnetic radiation by detecting resistance changes. Widely used to detect infrared and microwave radiation in telescopes and scientific instruments.

question: Avogadro's Law states that at same T and P, equal volumes of all gases contain:
information: Avogadro's Law: equal volumes of gases at same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules. This led to the mole concept. 1 mole of any gas at STP = 22.4 litres and contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules.

question: Ohm's Law states that:
information: Ohm's Law: V = IR where V = Voltage (Volts), I = Current (Amperes), R = Resistance (Ohms). Holds for ohmic conductors at constant temperature. Georg Simon Ohm formulated this in 1827.

question: The resistance of a wire is given by:
information: Ohm's Law: V = IR, so R = V/I. This holds for ohmic conductors (metals at constant temperature). Non-ohmic devices (diodes, thermistors) don't follow Ohm's Law.

question: In India, the AC supplied to homes has a frequency of:
information: India: domestic AC supply is 220 Volts at 50 Hz (50 cycles per second). USA: 110 V at 60 Hz. AC is used for homes because it can be efficiently transmitted at high voltage over long distances using transformers.

question: Which term does NOT denote electric power in a circuit?
information: Power P = VI = I²R = V²/R. I·R² has wrong dimensions (not Watts). So I·R² is the odd one out.

question: At the time of a short circuit, the current in the circuit:
information: In a short circuit, resistance drops to near zero. By Ohm's Law I = V/R → current becomes very large. This can melt wires, trip circuit breakers, or cause fires. Fuses and MCBs protect against short circuits.

question: What device protects electrical equipment from short circuits?
information: A fuse contains a thin wire that melts when excess current flows, breaking the circuit. Modern equivalents: MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) and RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker). Rated in Amperes (5A, 15A).

question: The lightning rod on buildings was invented by:
information: Benjamin Franklin invented the lightning rod in 1752. It is a tall metal rod connected to the ground — when lightning strikes, charge is safely conducted to ground protecting the building. Franklin also proved lightning is electrical.

question: Which device converts electrical energy into mechanical energy?
information: Electric Motor: electrical → mechanical energy (rotation). Generator/Dynamo: mechanical → electrical. Transformer only changes voltage (AC only); doesn't convert energy types. Motors are in fans, pumps, EVs.

question: Magnetic field is which type of quantity?
information: Magnetic field (B) has both magnitude and direction — it is a vector. Measured in Tesla (T). Earth's magnetic field ≈ 25–65 μT.

question: A Galvanometer is used to measure:
information: A galvanometer detects and measures small electric currents. Can be converted into: (a) Ammeter by adding low-resistance shunt in parallel, (b) Voltmeter by adding high resistance in series.

question: Hot-wire ammeters are used to measure:
information: Hot-wire ammeters work on heating effect of current — same for both AC and DC. Moving-coil ammeters work only for DC. Digital multimeters can measure both.

question: Which instrument is used for measuring electric potential (voltage)?
information: A potentiometer measures potential difference with zero current draw — ideal for accurate measurement. A voltmeter also measures voltage but draws small current. Barometer measures atmospheric pressure.

question: The device used to measure electric current is:
information: Ammeter measures electric current (Amperes). Connected in series; has very low resistance. Voltmeter measures voltage (connected in parallel). Voltameter (different!) is used for electrolysis.

question: The resistance of an ideal voltmeter is:
information: An ideal voltmeter has infinite resistance so no current flows through it — circuit is undisturbed. An ideal ammeter has zero resistance.

question: Which of the following radiations has the highest penetrating power?
information: Penetrating power: Gamma (γ) > X-rays > Beta (β) > Alpha (α). Alpha stopped by paper; Beta by aluminium; Gamma requires thick lead or concrete. However, alpha has the highest IONISING power.

question: The meter used to measure the distance moved by a vehicle is:
information: Odometer measures cumulative distance. Speedometer measures instantaneous speed. Chronometer = precise timekeeper. Tachometer = engine RPM.

question: Which instrument is used to measure humidity?
information: Hygrometer: humidity. Hydrometer: specific gravity of liquids. Pyrometer: very high temperatures (furnaces). Lactometer: purity/density of milk.

question: Electroencephalogram (EEG) is used for monitoring:
information: EEG records electrical activity of the brain. Used to diagnose epilepsy, sleep disorders. ECG (electrocardiogram) monitors the heart. EEG ≠ ECG — common confusion in exams.

question: Which of the following is used to measure the magnitude of an earthquake?
information: Richter Scale measures earthquake MAGNITUDE (energy released) — logarithmic scale (each step = 10× more energy). Mercalli Scale measures INTENSITY (damage/shaking felt by people). Seismograph is the instrument that records seismic waves.

question: Which instrument is used to measure atmospheric pressure?
information: Barometer measures atmospheric pressure. Invented by Torricelli in 1643 (mercury barometer). Thermometer: temperature. Hygrometer: humidity. Anemometer: wind speed. Seismograph: earthquake intensity.

question: Sphygmomanometer is used to measure:
information: Sphygmomanometer (BP apparatus) measures blood pressure. Normal BP = 120/80 mmHg. Stethoscope: heart and lung sounds. Thermometer: body temperature. Glucometer: blood glucose.

question: The molecular formula of Methyl Isocyanate (the Bhopal gas) is:
information: Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) = CH₃NCO. It leaked in the Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 2-3, 1984) at Union Carbide's pesticide plant — the world's worst industrial disaster. Killed thousands immediately; effects persist today.

question: The ore of Thorium is:
information: Monazite is the primary ore of Thorium. India has the world's largest Thorium reserves (mainly Kerala's coastal sands). Important for India's three-stage nuclear power programme. Pitchblende = Uranium ore.

question: The ore of Uranium is:
information: Pitchblende (UO₂) is the primary ore of Uranium. India's uranium deposits are in Jaduguda, Jharkhand. Monazite contains Thorium.

question: Which acid is secreted in the human stomach?
information: The stomach secretes HCl via parietal cells, maintaining gastric pH of 1.5–3.5. HCl activates pepsinogen to pepsin, kills bacteria, and aids protein digestion. Antacids neutralise excess HCl.

question: Which of the following conditions are suitable for liquefaction of gases?
information: Gases liquefy when cooled (reduced kinetic energy) and compressed (molecules brought closer). LPG, liquid nitrogen, and liquid oxygen are produced this way.

question: Which of the following is an iron ore?
information: Limonite (hydrated iron oxide) is an iron ore. Other iron ores: Magnetite (Fe₃O₄), Haematite (Fe₂O₃ — most important commercially). Cinnabar = Mercury ore. Bauxite = Aluminium ore.

question: Which component of fertiliser stimulates early/root growth?
information: NPK: Phosphorus promotes root development and early plant growth. Nitrogen promotes leafy green growth. Potassium promotes fruiting and disease resistance. DAP and SSP are phosphatic fertilisers.

question: The constituents of a soda-acid fire extinguisher are:
information: Soda-acid extinguisher: H₂SO₄ + NaHCO₃ → Na₂SO₄ + H₂O + CO₂↑. The CO₂ produced extinguishes fire by smothering (cuts off oxygen). Suitable for Class A fires (wood, paper).

question: The first artificially prepared (synthetic) element is:
information: Technetium (Tc, at. no. 43) was the first artificially synthesised element, in 1937 by Perrier and Segrè. Predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-manganese'. It is radioactive.

question: Which of the following has a highly elongated elliptical orbit?
information: Comets have highly elongated (eccentric) elliptical orbits. Halley's Comet: period ~76 years. When near Sun, they develop a bright tail pointing away from Sun. Asteroids orbit mainly in the asteroid belt (nearly circular orbits).

question: Which is the fluid component of blood?
information: Blood = Plasma (55%) + Formed elements (45%). Plasma is ~90% water + proteins + dissolved nutrients. Platelets help in clotting. RBCs contain haemoglobin for oxygen transport.

question: Corundum and cryolite are important ores of:
information: Corundum (Al₂O₃) and Cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) are aluminium ores. Bauxite is main commercial ore. Cryolite is used as flux in electrolytic reduction of alumina. Pure corundum = ruby/sapphire when coloured.

question: The pH value of 0.1N HCl solution is approximately:
information: 0.1N HCl → [H⁺] = 0.1 M = 10⁻¹ M. pH = −log[H⁺] = −log(10⁻¹) = 1. Gastric juice pH ≈ 1.5–3.5.

question: Which compound is used in contraceptive pills?
information: Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin used in oral contraceptive pills and emergency contraceptives. Cholecalciferol = Vitamin D₃. Venlafaxine = antidepressant. Cetirizine = antihistamine.

question: Why should oil not be stored in a galvanised iron pot?
choices: It causes corrosion of the pot
information: Zinc coating reacts with free fatty acids in oil and moisture, forming toxic zinc soaps. These reduce oil quality, cause discolouration, and lead to corrosion. Store food oils in stainless steel or glass.

question: What is the compound used as the 'night glow' (phosphorescent) pigment?
information: Europium-doped strontium aluminate (SrAl₂O₄:Eu²⁺, Dy³⁺) is the most efficient modern glow-in-the-dark material. Absorbs light and emits it slowly over hours. Used in safety signs, watch dials, escape routes.

question: Which metal compound is used for treatment of carcinoma (cancer)?
information: Cisplatin (cis-diamminedichloroplatinum(II)) is a platinum-based chemotherapy drug for testicular, ovarian, bladder, and lung carcinomas. Works by crosslinking DNA strands, preventing cancer cell replication.

question: Heat transfer that does NOT require a medium is called:
information: Radiation transfers heat via electromagnetic waves (infrared) — no medium required. Conduction: physical contact. Convection: fluid medium. The Sun heats Earth via radiation across the vacuum of space.

question: What type of rock is formed from cooling and solidification of lava?
information: Igneous rocks form from cooled magma/lava. Intrusive (slow cooling underground, large crystals, e.g., granite). Extrusive (fast cooling at surface, small crystals, e.g., basalt).

question: Burning of fossil fuels primarily causes:
answer: Air pollution (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter)
choices: Air pollution (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter)
information: Fossil fuel combustion releases CO₂ (greenhouse gas), SO₂ (acid rain), NOₓ, CO, and PM2.5/PM10. Primary cause of urban air pollution and climate change.

question: Air is classified as:
information: Air is a mixture of gases (N₂ 78%, O₂ 21%, Ar 0.93%, CO₂ 0.04%) — composition varies, components can be separated, no chemical change occurs. Compounds have fixed composition.

question: The highest percentage of gas in air is:
information: Air: Nitrogen 78.09%, Oxygen 20.95%, Argon 0.93%, CO₂ 0.04%. Many incorrectly think oxygen is most abundant — it is only 21%. Nitrogen dominates.

question: The major component of CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) is:
information: CNG is ~90-95% methane (CH₄). Cleaner fuel than petrol/diesel — produces less CO₂, no sulphur, less particulate matter. Used in buses, auto-rickshaws, taxis in Indian cities.

question: Inhalation of carbon monoxide (CO) is dangerous because:
answer: It combines with haemoglobin, preventing oxygen transport
choices: It combines with haemoglobin, preventing oxygen transport
It combines with excess oxygen in blood
information: CO has ~240 times higher affinity for haemoglobin than O₂, forming carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb) — cannot carry oxygen → tissue hypoxia and death. CO is colourless and odourless — making it especially dangerous.

question: The components of acid rain are:
information: Acid rain: SO₂ and NOₓ from fossil fuel burning react with water vapour to form H₂SO₄ and HNO₃. Lowers pH below 5.6. Damages forests, buildings, and aquatic life.

question: Which bacterial disease is called 'Splenic Fever'?
information: Anthrax (Bacillus anthracis) is called splenic fever — causes severe haemorrhagic enlargement of the spleen. Primarily affects livestock and can spread to humans. Also a potential bioterrorism agent.

question: Which one is an example of a thermosetting plastic (used for floor tiles)?
information: Melamine is a thermosetting plastic — once moulded under heat and pressure, cannot be re-melted. Used for crockery, floor/wall tiles, laminates (Formica). Thermoplastics (polythene, PVC, nylon) can be re-melted.

question: The major source of oxygen in the Earth's atmosphere is:
information: Oceanic phytoplankton produce ~50-80% of Earth's atmospheric oxygen. Called the 'lungs of the ocean'. Terrestrial plants contribute the rest.

question: A solution with pH = 7 is:
information: pH scale: 0-14. pH 7 = neutral (pure water). pH < 7 = acidic. pH > 7 = basic/alkaline. Blood pH ≈ 7.4 (slightly basic).

question: Which acid is present in vinegar?
information: Vinegar contains 5-8% acetic acid (ethanoic acid) in water. Citric acid = lemon/orange. Tartaric acid = tamarind/grapes. Lactic acid = curd.

question: Baking soda is chemically known as:
information: Baking soda = NaHCO₃. When heated or mixed with acid, releases CO₂ making dough rise. Washing soda = Na₂CO₃·10H₂O. Common salt = NaCl.

question: What colour does litmus paper turn in an acidic solution?
information: Litmus paper turns RED in acids and BLUE in bases. Litmus is a natural indicator from lichens. Neutral solutions: litmus remains purple/violet.

question: Plaster of Paris is obtained from:
information: Plaster of Paris (POP) = CaSO₄·½H₂O. Made by heating gypsum at 120-130°C. When mixed with water, sets hard (re-forms gypsum). Used in bone fracture casts, moulds, building decoration.

question: Galvanisation is the process of coating iron with:
information: Galvanisation coats iron/steel with zinc to prevent rusting. Zinc acts as a sacrificial anode — it oxidises preferentially protecting the iron underneath. Used in buckets, roofing sheets, pipes.

question: Stainless steel is an alloy of iron with:
information: Stainless steel = Iron + Chromium (~18%) + Nickel (~8%) + small amounts of Carbon. Chromium forms a passive oxide layer preventing rusting. Used in kitchen utensils, surgical instruments.

question: The hardest naturally occurring substance is:
information: Diamond is the hardest naturally occurring substance (10 on Mohs scale). Allotrope of carbon with each carbon bonded to 4 others in 3D tetrahedral structure. Corundum (Al₂O₃) is 9 on Mohs scale.

question: Which metal has the highest melting point and is used in electric bulb filaments?
information: Tungsten has the highest melting point of all metals (3422°C). Ideal for light bulb filaments which reach ~3000°C. Also used in X-ray tubes and rocket nozzles.

question: LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) is mainly composed of:
information: LPG = mixture of Propane (C₃H₈) and Butane (C₄H₁₀). Stored as liquid under pressure. Ethyl mercaptan (bad smell) is added to detect leaks. CNG is mainly Methane (CH₄).

question: Which gases are produced during fermentation of sugar by yeast?
information: Fermentation: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (by yeast). This is how bread rises (CO₂ gas) and alcohol is produced. Anaerobic process.

question: Dry ice is the solid form of:
information: Dry ice is solid CO₂. It sublimes at −78.5°C at atmospheric pressure (converts directly solid to gas) — no liquid stage. Used as refrigerant and in fog machines.

question: Nylon is a type of:
information: Nylon is the first fully synthetic fibre, invented by Wallace Carothers at DuPont (1935). A synthetic polyamide. Used in ropes, parachutes, stockings, toothbrush bristles.

question: The atmosphere is mainly heated by:
information: Sun emits short-wave radiation that passes through atmosphere and heats Earth's surface. The warmed surface re-emits long-wave (infrared) radiation, absorbed by greenhouse gases (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) — the greenhouse effect.

question: Nuclear fission is the basis of:
information: Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus (U-235) splits into smaller nuclei releasing enormous energy. Basis of atomic bomb and nuclear reactors. Nuclear fusion (hydrogen nuclei combining) = basis of hydrogen bomb and Sun's energy.

question: Carbon in its diamond form differs from graphite because:
answer: Diamond has a 3D tetrahedral lattice; graphite has layered hexagonal structure
choices: Diamond has a 3D tetrahedral lattice; graphite has layered hexagonal structure
Diamond is less dense than graphite
information: Both are allotropes of carbon. Diamond: each carbon bonded to 4 others in 3D lattice → hardest substance, non-conductor. Graphite: layered structure → soft, good electrical conductor. Used in pencils and electrodes.

question: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have:
answer: Same protons but different neutrons (different mass numbers)
choices: Same protons but different neutrons (different mass numbers)
Same mass number but different atomic number
information: Isotopes: Same atomic number (same element, same protons) but different mass numbers (different neutrons). Example: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 — both have 6 protons. Used in carbon dating and nuclear medicine.

question: The most abundant element in the Earth's crust is:
information: Earth's crust: Oxygen 46% > Silicon 28% > Aluminium 8% > Iron 5%. Most abundant in universe: Hydrogen. Most abundant in human body: Oxygen. Most abundant metal in crust: Aluminium.

question: Which is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature?
information: Mercury is the only metal liquid at room temperature (25°C). Bromine (non-metal) is the only non-metal liquid at room temperature. Gallium melts at 29.8°C (just above room temperature).

question: Noble gases (Group 18) are chemically inert because:
answer: They have completely filled outermost electron shells (stable octet)
choices: They have completely filled outermost electron shells (stable octet)
They do not exist as molecules
information: Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) have completely filled outermost shells — extremely stable and chemically unreactive. They exist as monoatomic gases. Used in light bulbs (Ar), neon signs (Ne), MRI (He).

question: What is the structural and functional unit of life?
information: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms — first described by Robert Hooke (1665). Cell Theory (Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow): all organisms are made of cells; cells arise from pre-existing cells.

question: Which is the non-contact force among the following?
information: Magnetic force acts at a distance without physical contact. Others: gravitational force, electrostatic force. Contact forces: friction, normal force, tension, impact.

question: The function of chlorophyll in photosynthesis is:
information: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy (mainly red and blue wavelengths, reflects green — hence plants appear green). The absorbed energy drives photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂.

question: Which part of the alimentary canal receives bile from the liver?
information: Bile is produced in the liver, stored in the gall bladder, and released into the duodenum via the bile duct. Bile emulsifies fats for lipase to act on.

question: The process of breaking down organic matter by micro-organisms is called:
information: Decomposition breaks dead organic matter into simpler inorganic substances by decomposers (bacteria, fungi). Recycles nutrients in ecosystems. Fermentation is a specific type of anaerobic decomposition.

question: Who was the first to propose a model for the structure of an atom?
information: J.J. Thomson proposed the 'Plum Pudding Model' (1904) — electrons embedded in positive sphere. Rutherford's nuclear model (1911) came next, followed by Bohr's planetary model (1913).

question: Which is the fundamental physical quantity?
information: The 7 fundamental (base) quantities: length, mass, time, electric current, temperature, luminous intensity, amount of substance. Force, velocity, and work are all derived quantities.

question: Hormones are secreted by:
information: Endocrine glands (ductless) secrete hormones directly into bloodstream. Examples: pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, pancreas. Exocrine glands have ducts (e.g., sweat glands, salivary glands).

question: Xylem in plants is responsible for:
answer: Transport of water and dissolved minerals (upward)
choices: Transport of water and dissolved minerals (upward)
information: Xylem transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves (upward). Phloem transports food/sugars from leaves to other parts (bidirectional). Together they form the vascular system.

question: Which of the following is a Kharif crop?
information: Kharif crops: sown at monsoon start (June-July), harvested in autumn — rice, maize, cotton, jowar, bajra, sugarcane, soybean. Rabi crops: sown in winter (Oct-Nov), harvested in spring — wheat, barley, gram, mustard.

question: The edible part of Mango is:
information: Drupe fruit: Epicarp (outer skin) + Mesocarp (fleshy edible part) + Endocarp (hard stone enclosing seed). In mango, we eat the mesocarp. In coconut: we eat endosperm.

question: The species in immediate danger of extinction are known as:
information: IUCN Red List: Extinct → Extinct in Wild → Critically Endangered → Endangered → Vulnerable → Near Threatened → Least Concern. Endangered = very high risk; Vulnerable = high risk but not yet endangered.

question: In mycorrhizal association, the advantage to the plant is:
answer: Increased mineral absorption and disease protection
choices: Increased mineral absorption and disease protection
information: Mycorrhizae: symbiotic fungi + plant roots. Plants provide carbohydrates to fungi; fungi provide enhanced mineral absorption (especially phosphorus), protection against root pathogens, and drought resistance.

question: Ozone Day is celebrated on:
information: World Ozone Day = 16 September — anniversary of the Montreal Protocol (1987) which phased out CFCs. Ozone layer protects from harmful UV-B radiation. World Environment Day = 5 June.

question: Who first observed cells under a microscope and coined the term 'cell'?
information: Robert Hooke (1665) observed cork under a microscope and called the structures 'cells' (from Latin 'cella' = small room). Anton van Leeuwenhoek later observed living microorganisms (bacteria, protozoa).

question: The 'powerhouse of the cell' is the:
information: Mitochondria produce ATP (adenosine triphosphate) — the energy currency of the cell — through aerobic respiration. They have their own DNA (evidence of endosymbiotic origin).

question: Which cell organelle is responsible for protein synthesis?
information: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis in all cells. They read mRNA and assemble amino acids into proteins. Found in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. Called the 'factory of the cell'.

question: The process of photosynthesis takes place in which organelle?
information: Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts: thylakoid membranes (light reactions) and stroma (Calvin cycle/dark reactions). Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll. Only in plant cells.

question: Cell wall in plant cells is mainly composed of:
information: Plant cell walls = cellulose (polysaccharide). Fungal cell walls = chitin. Bacterial cell walls = peptidoglycan. Animal cells do NOT have a cell wall.

question: The largest organ of the human body is:
information: Skin is the largest organ (by surface area and weight, ~2 sq metres). The liver is the largest INTERNAL organ. Skin functions: protection, temperature regulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis.

question: How many bones are there in an adult human body?
information: Adult human: 206 bones. Newborn: ~270-300 bones (fuse during development). Smallest bone: stapes (ear). Longest bone: femur (thigh bone). Most bones: in the hands and feet.

question: Universal blood donor is blood group:
information: O negative (O−) = universal donor (lacks ABO antigens and Rh factor — can be given to anyone). AB positive (AB+) = universal recipient (can receive any blood type).

question: Which organ produces insulin in the human body?
information: Pancreas (islets of Langerhans) produces insulin (lowers blood glucose) and glucagon (raises blood glucose). Diabetes mellitus: pancreas doesn't produce enough insulin or body can't use it properly.

question: The 'master gland' of the human endocrine system is the:
information: Pituitary gland (at base of brain) controls most other endocrine glands through hormones. Size of a pea. Anterior pituitary: secretes GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH. Posterior: ADH, oxytocin.

question: Goitre is caused by deficiency of which mineral?
information: Goitre = enlargement of the thyroid gland due to iodine deficiency. Thyroid needs iodine to produce thyroxine. Solution: use iodised salt. Iron deficiency = anaemia.

question: Deficiency of Vitamin C causes which disease?
information: Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) deficiency → Scurvy (bleeding gums, slow wound healing, weakness). Vitamin D → Rickets. Vitamin B1 → Beriberi. Vitamin A → Night blindness.

question: Rickets in children is caused by deficiency of:
information: Rickets = Vitamin D deficiency — bones become soft and deformed due to poor calcium absorption. Vitamin D is produced by skin on exposure to sunlight (UV rays). In adults, same deficiency causes Osteomalacia.

question: Which vitamins are fat-soluble?
information: Fat-soluble vitamins: A, D, E, K (remembered as ADEK). Stored in body fat and liver. Water-soluble vitamins: B-complex and C — must be consumed regularly as they are excreted in urine.

question: Malaria is caused by a protozoan parasite called:
information: Malaria: Plasmodium parasite (P. falciparum most deadly), transmitted by Female Anopheles mosquito. Symptoms: recurring fever, chills, anaemia. Drug: Quinine/Chloroquine/Artemisinin.

question: Kala-azar (Visceral Leishmaniasis) is endemic in Bihar. It is transmitted by:
information: Kala-azar: caused by Leishmania donovani (protozoan), transmitted by bite of Female Phlebotomus argentipes sandfly. Endemic in Bihar, Jharkhand, UP, WB. Symptoms: prolonged fever, enlarged spleen/liver, skin darkening. Bihar BPSC high-relevance topic.

question: AIDS is caused by:
information: AIDS (Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome): HIV attacks CD4+ T-helper lymphocytes, destroying the immune system. Transmitted through blood, sexual contact, or mother to child. No cure; managed with Antiretroviral (ARV) drugs.

question: Dengue fever is transmitted by:
information: Dengue: viral disease transmitted by Aedes aegypti (bites during the day, unlike malaria mosquito which bites at night). Platelet count drops severely. No specific treatment — supportive care.

question: Penicillin, the first antibiotic, was discovered by:
information: Alexander Fleming discovered Penicillin in 1928 — noticed Penicillium mould killed bacteria on a culture plate. Fleming, Florey and Chain won the 1945 Nobel Prize in Medicine.

question: The 'Father of Genetics' is:
information: Gregor Mendel (1822-1884), an Austrian monk, formulated the laws of inheritance through pea plant experiments: Law of Segregation and Law of Independent Assortment. His work was rediscovered in 1900.

question: The double helix structure of DNA was determined in 1953 by:
information: James Watson and Francis Crick determined the double helix structure of DNA in 1953, using X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin. Won Nobel Prize in Physiology (1962). Franklin's contribution is now fully recognised.

question: The theory of 'Natural Selection' as the mechanism of evolution was proposed by:
information: Charles Darwin proposed evolution by Natural Selection in 1859 in 'On the Origin of Species'. Key idea: individuals with favourable traits survive and reproduce more (survival of the fittest). Alfred Wallace independently reached similar conclusions.

question: Chikungunya is caused by:
information: Chikungunya virus (CHIKV) belongs to genus Alphavirus, family Togaviridae. RNA virus. Disease causes severe joint pain ('chikungunya' = 'that which bends up' in Makonde language).

question: Chikungunya virus is primarily transmitted by which mosquito?
information: Aedes aegypti (primary) and Aedes albopictus (tiger mosquito) transmit Chikungunya. Same mosquitoes transmit Dengue and Zika. Anopheles = Malaria. Culex = Japanese Encephalitis.

question: The most characteristic symptom of Chikungunya is:
information: Chikungunya's hallmark: debilitating joint pain (arthralgia) persisting weeks to months. Other symptoms: high fever, rash, headache. Unlike Dengue, Chikungunya rarely causes haemorrhage or death.

question: Which disease is NOT transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes?
information: Malaria is transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito — not Aedes. Aedes aegypti/albopictus transmit: Dengue, Chikungunya, Zika, Yellow Fever. This distinction is frequently tested.

question: Which of the following is the natural reservoir of Nipah virus?
information: Fruit bats (Pteropus genus) are natural hosts of Nipah virus and remain asymptomatic. Humans infected through: direct bat contact, bat-contaminated date palm sap, infected pigs, or human-to-human transmission.

question: Persistent infection with which virus is the primary cause of cervical cancer?
information: HPV (especially types 16 and 18) causes ~70-85% of cervical cancers. HPV vaccination (Gardasil, Cervarix) provides protection. India launched a national HPV vaccination program.

question: Which HPV types are considered high-risk for cervical cancer?
information: HPV 16 and 18 are the most dangerous oncogenic strains — responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. HPV 6 and 11 cause genital warts (low-risk). HPV 1 and 2 cause common skin warts.

question: Edward Jenner is associated with:
information: Edward Jenner (1749-1823) developed the first vaccine against smallpox in 1796 using cowpox material. Called 'Father of Immunology'. Louis Pasteur developed vaccines against rabies and anthrax and established germ theory.

question: What is the thin layer of gases surrounding the Earth called?
information: Atmosphere: layer of gases (up to ~10,000 km). Biosphere: zone of life. Hydrosphere: all water bodies. Lithosphere: solid outer crust. Atmospheric layers: Troposphere → Stratosphere → Mesosphere → Thermosphere → Exosphere.

question: Environment is a composite state of:
answer: All of these (biotic + abiotic + physiographic factors)
choices: All of these (biotic + abiotic + physiographic factors)
information: Environment encompasses: Biotic factors (living organisms), Abiotic factors (temperature, water, soil, light), and Physiographic factors (terrain, altitude, climate).

question: In which year was the International Space Station (ISS) launched?
information: ISS construction began November 1998 with the Zarya module. Joint project by NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA. First crewed stay: November 2000. Orbits at ~400 km altitude.

question: The world's first space station was:
information: Salyut 1 launched by USSR on April 19, 1971 — world's first space station. Skylab (USA, 1973) was second. Mir (USSR/Russia, 1986-2001) was most successful early station. China's Tiangong is newest (2021).

question: The first country to launch a space station was:
information: USSR launched Salyut 1 on April 19, 1971 — world's first space station. USA's first = Skylab (1973). China's = Tiangong-1 (2011). India does not yet have a space station.

question: The first person in the world to travel to space was:
information: Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1961 — Vostok 1 mission. Alan Shepard = first American (May 5, 1961). Rakesh Sharma = first Indian in space (1984). Shubhanshu Shukla = India's upcoming astronaut for Axiom Mission 4 (2025).

question: Ozone layer depletion is mainly caused by:
information: CFCs (used in refrigerators, ACs, aerosol sprays) release chlorine atoms in the stratosphere that catalytically destroy ozone (O₃). Montreal Protocol (1987) was signed to phase out CFCs. The ozone hole is mainly over Antarctica.

question: India's first satellite was:
information: Aryabhata was India's first satellite, launched April 19, 1975 by the Soviet Kosmos-3M rocket. Named after ancient Indian mathematician Aryabhata. India's first indigenously LAUNCHED satellite = Rohini (1980) by SLV-3.

question: Chandrayaan-3 made India the first country to land a spacecraft near the Moon's:
information: Chandrayaan-3's Vikram lander made a successful soft landing near the Moon's south pole on August 23, 2023 — making India the 4th country to land on the Moon and the FIRST to land near the south pole (a scientifically significant region with possible water ice).

question: The indigenous malaria vaccine candidate developed under ICMR is called:
information: AdFalciVax is India's indigenous malaria vaccine candidate developed by ICMR and Bharat Biotech. R21/Matrix-M (Oxford) and RTS,S/AS01 (GSK) are WHO-approved malaria vaccines. Covaxin = India's COVID-19 vaccine.

question: AdFalciVax is primarily targeted against which malaria parasite?
information: Plasmodium falciparum is the most deadly malaria parasite causing majority of malaria deaths globally. P. vivax is more common but less fatal.

question: What is the typical electrical output range of Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)?
information: SMRs are defined as nuclear reactors with capacity up to 300 MWe. Factory-built and assembled on-site — reducing construction time and cost. India's NPCIL is developing Bharat Small Modular Reactor (BSMR) designs.

question: What is the key feature of SMRs compared to traditional nuclear reactors?
answer: Can be manufactured in a factory and assembled on-site
choices: Can be manufactured in a factory and assembled on-site
information: SMRs are modular (factory-fabricated, transportable), scalable, and have enhanced passive safety features. Suitable for remote areas and industrial heat applications. Crucial for India's carbon-neutral goals.

question: CRISPR-Cas9 technology is originally derived from:
information: CRISPR is a natural defense mechanism in bacteria that cuts and stores fragments of viral DNA. Scientists repurposed Cas9 enzyme for programmable genome editing. Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier won Nobel Prize 2020.

question: CRISPR in gene editing stands for:
answer: Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats
choices: Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats
Circular RNA Sequence for Protein Regulation
Controlled Response System for Precision Repair
Combined Replication System for Plant Research
information: CRISPR acts as molecular 'scissors' — Cas9 enzyme cuts DNA at specific sequences guided by RNA. Applications: sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, cancer immunotherapy, crop improvement.

question: Which nutrient is Makhana (Fox Nut) particularly rich in?
information: Makhana (Euryale ferox) is rich in protein (~9-10g per 100g), magnesium, potassium, and is low in fat. Bihar produces ~85% of India's makhana — mainly in Mithila region. Mithila Makhana has GI tag.

question: What is the botanical name of the Makhana plant?
information: Euryale ferox is the prickly water lily whose seeds are Makhana/Fox Nuts. Grows in ponds and wetlands. Nelumbo nucifera is lotus (different plant, also produces edible seeds).

question: Which of the following is the natural reservoir of Nipah virus?
information: Fruit bats (Pteropus genus) are natural hosts of Nipah virus, remaining asymptomatic. Humans infected through: direct bat contact, bat-contaminated date palm sap, infected pigs, or human-to-human transmission.

question: Who invented the telephone?
information: Alexander Graham Bell invented the telephone in 1876. Thomas Edison invented the electric bulb (1879), phonograph, and motion picture camera. Marconi invented radio (1895). Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction.

question: X-rays were discovered by:
answer: Wilhelm Röntgen (1895) — won first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901)
choices: Wilhelm Röntgen (1895) — won first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901)
information: Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays on November 8, 1895 — accidentally, while experimenting with cathode ray tubes. Awarded the first-ever Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901. His wife's hand was the first X-ray image.
